summary: The post uses Google Flu Trends as a cautionary tale about the dangers of making predictions when the underlying measurement tools and landscape are constantly changing. GFT's algorithm failed because it depended on Google Search behavior remaining stable, which it didn't. The author extends this lesson to the startup world, arguing that data-driven predictions about startup success are similarly unreliable because the internet, fundraising norms, founder archetypes, and success metrics all evolve rapidly. She also highlights the lack of accountability for failed predictions, since people forget unsuccessful startups quickly. The core argument is that in fast-moving industries, historical data is an unreliable guide to the future.
key_points: Google Flu Trends failed because its algorithm depended on Google Search remaining stable, but search algorithms and user behavior changed significantly over time
Startup success criteria are volatile — what makes a good founder, how companies raise money, and which metrics matter all shift rapidly
There is a dangerous lack of accountability for data-driven predictions, since failed startups are forgotten quickly while lucky successes are rationalized after the fact
The 'next big thing' almost by definition defies existing predictive models, making historical pattern-matching unreliable
Metrics themselves are unstable — social metrics didn't exist a decade ago, and companies can now be worth billions with zero revenue
money_quotes: We don't know what will make the next startup successful based on what's worked in the past, because the standards for success change so rapidly.
Almost by definition, the 'next big thing' is going to be something we aren't prepared for.
We don't know what the best metrics are to measure the next generation of startups, because we don't yet know how they will be measured.
Revenue probably once sounded like a reliable metric 20 years ago, but now, it's possible for a company to have zero revenue and be valued at over $1B.
If a startup defies the algorithm, we just say they got lucky.
The ground is moving beneath our feet even as we try to build upon it.
Perhaps nowhere else are algorithms for success more volatile than in technology-based startups.
key_insight: Data-driven predictions break down in rapidly evolving industries because the underlying landscape, measurement tools, and success criteria are all shifting simultaneously, yet there is little accountability when those predictions fail.
